questions.question:
Consider a schedule of transactions T_1 and T_2:

T_1: RA  RC  WA  WB
T_2:   RB   WB     RD  WC   Commit

(T_1 does not Commit in the original schedule; T_2 Commits)

Here, RX stands for "Read(X)" and WX stands for "Write(X)". Which one of the following schedules is conflict equivalent to the above schedule?

A.
T_2: RA  RC  WB  WC  Commit
T_1: RB  WA       RD     WB  Commit

B.
T_2: RA  RC      WA  WB
T_1:   RB  WB  RD     WC  Commit

C.
T_1: RA  RC  WA  WB
T_2:   RB  WB  RA  WC  RD  WD  Commit

D.
T_1: RA  RC  WA  WB
T_2:   RB  WB     RD  WC  Commit
questions.answer:
D. T_1: RA  RC  WA  WB
T_2:   RB  WB     RD  WC  Commit
questions.to_find: Which schedule is conflict equivalent to the given schedule of T_1 and T_2

understand.plain: Two transactions T_1 and T_2 are interleaved in a given schedule. Two schedules are conflict equivalent if one can be transformed into the other by swapping adjacent non-conflicting operations. We must identify which option preserves all conflicting pairs in the same relative order as the original schedule.

keywords.explain: Two operations conflict if they belong to different transactions, access the same data item, and at least one is a Write.
keywords.example: T_1 writing A and T_2 reading A conflict; T_1 reading A and T_2 reading A do not conflict.

keywords.explain: Two schedules are conflict equivalent if they involve the same transactions with the same operations and every pair of conflicting operations appears in the same relative order.
keywords.example: If T_1:WA comes before T_2:RA in schedule S1, then in any conflict-equivalent schedule, T_1:WA must also come before T_2:RA.

keywords.explain: A schedule that is conflict equivalent to some serial schedule (no interleaving).
keywords.example: If the precedence graph is acyclic the schedule is conflict serializable.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='520' height='200' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='35' class='lbl ink'>Original Schedule</text><text x='20' y='65' class='sub ink'>T1: RA -- RC -- WA -- WB</text><text x='20' y='90' class='sub ink'>T2:    RB    WB      RD -- WC -- Commit</text><text x='20' y='130' class='lbl ink'>Conflict Pairs (order must be preserved)</text><text x='20' y='158' class='sub mut'>T2:RB before T1:WB  |  T1:WA before T2: (none on A)  |  T2:WB before T1: (none)</text><text x='20' y='180' class='sub mut'>Option D preserves exactly the same interleaving structure</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The original schedule shows T1 doing RA RC WA WB and T2 interleaved doing RB WB RD WC Commit. Conflict equivalent means all conflicting operation pairs maintain the same relative order.

given.aim: Find the schedule conflict equivalent to the given interleaved schedule of T_1 and T_2.

terms.meaning: T_1: RA RC WA WB interleaved with T_2: RB WB RD WC Commit
terms.example: T_1 reads A, T_2 reads B, T_1 reads C, T_2 writes B, T_1 writes A, T_1 writes B, T_2 reads D, T_2 writes C
terms.connects: We must identify all conflicting pairs and their order to match against each option.

terms.meaning: Pairs of operations from different transactions on the same data item where at least one is a Write
terms.example: T_2:WB and T_1:WB conflict since both write B; T_2:RB and T_1:WB conflict
terms.connects: The relative order of these pairs must be identical in any conflict-equivalent schedule.

terms.meaning: To go from one conflict-equivalent schedule to another, only non-conflicting adjacent operations may be swapped
terms.example: T_1:RC and T_2:RB can be swapped since both are reads on different items
terms.connects: This is the mechanism to check equivalence.

given.plan: List all conflicting pairs in the original schedule and note their relative order. Then check each option to see if those same pairs appear in the same relative order. The option that matches is conflict equivalent.

steps.title: Identify conflicting pairs in original schedule
steps.formula_raw: Two ops conflict if: different transactions, same data item, at least one Write
steps.apply:
Original order: T1:RA, T2:RB, T1:RC, T2:WB, T1:WA, T1:WB, T2:RD, T2:WC.
Conflicting pairs:
(1) T2:WB vs T1:WB -> T2:WB comes BEFORE T1:WB
(2) T2:RB vs T1:WB -> T2:RB comes BEFORE T1:WB
No conflict on A (T2 does not access A), no conflict on C (T1 does not access C in a write that overlaps T2:WC on item C -- but T1:RC and T2:WC do conflict: T1:RC before T2:WC).
Full list: T2:RB < T1:WB, T2:WB < T1:WB (T2 writes B first), T1:RC < T2:WC.
steps.note: Carefully check every data item accessed by both transactions.

steps.title: Check option D
steps.apply:
Option D schedule: T1:RA, T2:RB, T1:RC, T2:WB, T1:WA, T1:WB, T2:RD, T2:WC, Commit.
This is exactly the same interleaving as the original schedule (T_2 gets a Commit appended but all operations are identical). All conflicting pairs have the same order. This is trivially conflict equivalent.
steps.note: Options A, B, C either change the transaction operations or reorder conflicting pairs, so they are NOT conflict equivalent.

solution.result: Option D is conflict equivalent to the given schedule.

questions.formula_note: Conflict equivalence: two schedules are equivalent iff every pair of conflicting operations appears in the same relative order. Checked by listing all conflicting pairs and comparing their ordering across schedules.

questions.question:
Suppose a database system crashes again while recovering from a previous crash. Assume checkpointing is not done by the database either during the transactions or during recovery. Which of the following statements is/are correct?

A. The same undo and redo list will be used while recovering again
B. The system cannot recover any further
C. All the transactions that are already undone and redone will not be recovered again
D. The database will become inconsistent
questions.to_find: Which statements about crash-during-recovery (without checkpointing) are correct

understand.plain: A database was executing transactions and crashed. During recovery from that crash, the system crashed again. There is no checkpointing. We need to determine what happens when recovery is attempted again after this second crash. The key insight is that without checkpoints, recovery always re-reads the entire log from the beginning.

keywords.explain: Set of transactions that were committed before the crash and whose effects must be reapplied (redone) to make the database reflect those commits.
keywords.example: If T1 committed but its writes may not have reached disk, T1 is on the redo list.

keywords.explain: Set of transactions that were active (not committed) at the time of crash and whose partial effects must be rolled back.
keywords.example: If T2 had written some data but never committed when the crash happened, T2 is on the undo list.

keywords.explain: A periodic operation that flushes dirty pages and writes a checkpoint record, allowing recovery to start from the checkpoint rather than the beginning of the log.
keywords.example: With a checkpoint at time t, recovery only needs to process log entries after t.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='520' height='180' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='35' class='lbl ink'>Timeline</text><rect x='20' y='55' width='480' height='8' rx='4' class='mut'/><circle cx='60' cy='59' r='8' class='ok'/><text x='55' y='88' class='sub mut'>Start</text><circle cx='220' cy='59' r='8' class='no'/><text x='205' y='88' class='sub mut'>Crash 1</text><circle cx='360' cy='59' r='8' class='no'/><text x='345' y='88' class='sub mut'>Crash 2</text><text x='20' y='120' class='sub ink'>No checkpoints: recovery always starts from log beginning</text><text x='20' y='145' class='sub ink'>=> Same undo/redo list computed both times (A is correct)</text><text x='20' y='168' class='sub ink'>System CAN recover (B is wrong); idempotent redo/undo</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A timeline showing original execution, then crash 1, then recovery attempt, then crash 2. Without checkpoints, each recovery re-reads the entire log and computes the same undo/redo lists.

given.aim: Determine which statements correctly describe the behavior of crash recovery after a crash-during-recovery, with no checkpointing.

terms.meaning: The log is never truncated; recovery must always re-read from the start of the log
terms.example: Whether it's the first crash or the second crash, the recovery manager reads the full log.
terms.connects: This means the undo and redo lists will be identical on each recovery attempt.

terms.meaning: Applying redo or undo multiple times to the same operation yields the same result as applying it once
terms.example: Redoing a Write(X, 50) twice still leaves X = 50.
terms.connects: Even if some redo/undo was partially done before crash 2, re-doing them again is safe.

terms.meaning: The system uses the write-ahead log to reconstruct the database state after a crash
terms.example: The log records before-images (for undo) and after-images (for redo) of every write.
terms.connects: As long as the log is intact, recovery is always possible regardless of how many crashes occur.

given.plan: Analyze each option: without checkpoints, the entire log is re-scanned from the beginning each time, producing the same undo and redo sets. Redo and undo operations are idempotent, so recovery is always possible. The system will not become inconsistent as long as the log is intact.

steps.title: Determine undo/redo lists without checkpoints
steps.formula_raw: Redo list = {T | T committed before crash}; Undo list = {T | T was active at crash}
steps.apply: Without checkpoints, recovery always starts from the very beginning of the log. The undo and redo lists are determined solely by which transactions committed vs. which were active at the time of the ORIGINAL crash. The second crash (during recovery) does not change which transactions were in the log. Therefore, when recovering from crash 2, the same log is read from the start and the SAME undo/redo lists are computed. Statement A is CORRECT.
steps.note: The crash during recovery does not add new committed or aborted transactions to the log (recovery writes undo log records, but the original transaction set remains the same).

steps.title: Evaluate remaining options
steps.apply:
B: 'System cannot recover any further' - FALSE. As long as the log is intact and durable, recovery is always possible. Redo/undo are idempotent.
C: 'Transactions already undone/redone will not be recovered again' - FALSE. Without checkpoints, the system re-processes the entire log, so it WILL redo/undo them again (idempotently).
D: 'Database will become inconsistent' - FALSE. The recovery process is designed to handle crashes during recovery; idempotency ensures correctness.

solution.result: Only statement A is correct: the same undo and redo list will be used while recovering again.

questions.formula_note: Log-based crash recovery: without checkpointing, the full log is always re-scanned from the start, yielding the same undo/redo lists; redo and undo operations are idempotent, so multiple crashes during recovery are handled correctly.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements about the Two Phase Locking (2PL) protocol is/are TRUE?

A. 2PL permits only serializable schedules
B. With 2PL, a transaction always locks the data item being read or written just before every operation and always releases the lock just after the operation
C. With 2PL, once a lock is released by a transaction, the transaction can acquire no new locks
D. A deadlock is possible with 2PL
questions.to_find: Which statements about the Two Phase Locking (2PL) protocol are TRUE

understand.plain: Two Phase Locking (2PL) is a concurrency control protocol that divides a transaction's lock operations into two phases: a growing phase (where locks are acquired but never released) and a shrinking phase (where locks are released but no new locks can be acquired). We need to identify which statements about 2PL are correct.

keywords.explain: A protocol where a transaction must not acquire any new lock after it has released any lock. This ensures conflict serializability.
keywords.example: T1 acquires lock(A), lock(B), releases lock(A) -- now T1 cannot acquire any new locks.

keywords.explain: The first phase of 2PL where a transaction may acquire locks but may not release any locks.
keywords.example: T1: lock(A), lock(B), lock(C) -- all acquisitions, no releases yet.

keywords.explain: The second phase of 2PL where a transaction may release locks but may not acquire new ones.
keywords.example: T1: unlock(A), unlock(B), unlock(C) -- all releases after the lock point.

keywords.explain: A situation where two or more transactions are each waiting for a lock held by the other, forming a cycle.
keywords.example: T1 holds lock(A) waiting for lock(B); T2 holds lock(B) waiting for lock(A).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='520' height='200' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='30' class='lbl ink'>2PL: Growing vs Shrinking Phase</text><rect x='20' y='50' width='200' height='40' rx='6' class='ok'/><text x='120' y='76' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' style='fill:white'>Growing Phase</text><rect x='240' y='50' width='260' height='40' rx='6' class='no'/><text x='370' y='76' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' style='fill:white'>Shrinking Phase</text><text x='220' y='115' class='big ink' text-anchor='middle'>|</text><text x='220' y='140' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Lock Point (max locks held)</text><text x='20' y='170' class='sub ink'>After lock point: NO new locks allowed (Statement C is TRUE)</text><text x='20' y='190' class='sub ink'>2PL => serializable (A is TRUE); deadlock still possible (D is TRUE)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing the two phases of 2PL: growing phase where locks are acquired, and shrinking phase where locks are released. The lock point is the maximum, after which no new locks can be acquired.

given.aim: Identify all true statements about the Two Phase Locking (2PL) protocol.

terms.meaning: 2PL ensures that every schedule it produces is conflict serializable
terms.example: Any schedule allowed by 2PL can be shown equivalent to some serial schedule.
terms.connects: Statement A claims 2PL permits only serializable schedules -- this is TRUE.

terms.meaning: In basic 2PL, a transaction acquires all needed locks first (growing phase), then releases them; it does NOT lock-just-before and unlock-just-after every operation
terms.example: T1 can hold lock(A) while performing operations on B, C, etc.
terms.connects: Statement B describes lock coupling (not standard 2PL), so B is FALSE.

terms.meaning: Despite enforcing serializability, 2PL does not prevent deadlocks because transactions may hold locks and wait for others
terms.example: T1 holds lock(A) wants lock(B); T2 holds lock(B) wants lock(A) -- deadlock under 2PL.
terms.connects: Statement D claims deadlock is possible with 2PL -- this is TRUE.

given.plan: Evaluate each statement against the definition and properties of basic 2PL. A is true (2PL guarantees conflict serializability). B is false (2PL does not require locking just before and unlocking just after each operation -- that would be a different protocol). C is true (once a lock is released, no new locks can be acquired -- this IS the 2PL rule). D is true (2PL does not prevent deadlocks).

steps.title: Evaluate statement A: 2PL permits only serializable schedules
steps.apply: This is the fundamental theorem of 2PL: any schedule allowed by the 2PL protocol is conflict serializable. The proof uses the fact that 2PL transactions can be serialized in the order of their lock points. Statement A is TRUE.
steps.note: The converse is not true -- not every serializable schedule can be produced by 2PL, but 2PL is sufficient for serializability.

steps.title: Evaluate statement B: lock just before, unlock just after every operation
steps.apply: This describes a lock-unlock-per-operation approach, NOT 2PL. In 2PL, once the first lock is released (entering shrinking phase), no new locks may be acquired. The description in B (unlock just after operation, lock just before next operation) would allow re-acquiring locks, violating the 2PL rule. Statement B is FALSE.
steps.note: The approach described in B is called 'lock coupling' or 'crabbing', used in B-tree concurrency, not standard 2PL.

steps.title: Evaluate statements C and D
steps.apply:
C: 'Once a lock is released, the transaction can acquire no new locks' -- this is the DEFINITION of 2PL (the two-phase rule). Statement C is TRUE.
D: 'A deadlock is possible with 2PL' -- 2PL prevents cascading rollbacks (in Strict 2PL) and ensures serializability, but it does NOT prevent deadlocks. Two transactions can each hold a lock the other needs, causing a cycle. Statement D is TRUE.
steps.note: Strict 2PL holds all locks until commit to prevent cascading rollbacks, but neither basic 2PL nor strict 2PL prevents deadlocks.

solution.result: Statements A, C, and D are TRUE. Statement B is FALSE.

questions.formula_note: Two Phase Locking theorem: any schedule produced by 2PL is conflict serializable (growing phase followed by shrinking phase with the lock point in between); 2PL does not prevent deadlocks.
